item.title: 英語学習 listening_20260615
item.description:
# ════════════════════════════════════════ # Listening Material — 2026/06/15 # ════════════════════════════════════════
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # TOPIC 1: B4歓迎会・寿司の大量注文 # ────────────────────────────────────────
# [Casual] We're having a welcome party for the new B4 students today! Haruna and I set everything up. We ordered a ton of sushi because, hey, organizer's privilege — gotta make it count. Can't wait!
# [Professional] Today we're hosting a welcome reception for the incoming B4 students. Haruna and I handled the planning, and we took full advantage of our organizer roles to order plenty of sushi. Looking forward to it.
# [Super Native] So we've got the B4 welcome thing today — Haruna and I ran the whole show. Ordered way too much sushi on purpose. Organizer perks, you know? Stoked.
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # TOPIC 2: B4歓迎会の準備（SPEAK MODE） # ────────────────────────────────────────
# [修正版] Today we're having a welcome reception for the B4 students. Haruna and I took care of all the prep.
# ここがポイントです。 # 「確定済みの近未来の予定」には will より現在進行形 be + V-ing が自然です。また、自分を含む場合は I を最後に置くのがマナーです。
Today we're having a welcome reception for the B4 students.
# [ネイティブ代替] Haruna and I put the whole thing together for the B4 welcome party today.
# ここがポイントです。 # put ~ together は「一から企画・準備した」という労力と主体性を自然に含む表現です。took care of the prep より能動的な印象を与えます。
Haruna and I put the whole thing together for the B4 welcome party today.
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # TOPIC 3: ワールドカップ寝落ち（SPEAK MODE） # ────────────────────────────────────────
# [修正版] Today, at 5 AM, the Japan vs. Netherlands World Cup match was held. I'd been really looking forward to it and tried to stay up, but by 4 AM I just couldn't keep it up and fell asleep. I ended up missing the whole thing.
# [ネイティブ代替] I was hyped for the Japan-Netherlands match at 5 AM, but I crashed around 4 and missed the whole game.
# ここがポイントです。 # hyped は「興奮して楽しみにしていた」、crashed は「そのまま寝落ちした」を表す口語表現です。2文を1文に圧縮してテンポよく状況が伝わります。
I was hyped for the Japan-Netherlands match at 5 AM, but I crashed around 4 and missed the whole game.
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # TOPIC 4: stay up vs stayed late — 使い分け # ────────────────────────────────────────
I tried to stay up for the match. I stayed late at the office to finish the report.
# ここがポイントです。 # stay up は「夜更かしして起きていようとする」、stayed late は「夜遅くまで（場所に）いた」という意味です。起きていようとする意図には stay up を使います。
I tried to stay up for the match.
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # TOPIC 5: keep it up vs keep up — 使い分け # ────────────────────────────────────────
I couldn't keep it up. I couldn't keep up with the pace.
# ここがポイントです。 # keep it up はある状態を維持し続けること、keep up with は何かについていくこと、という別の意味になります。
I couldn't keep it up.
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # TOPIC 6: ended up missing — 結果表現 # ────────────────────────────────────────
I ended up missing the whole thing. I ended up falling asleep on the couch. I ended up staying home instead.
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # TOPIC 7: 単語・フレーズ例文 # ────────────────────────────────────────
# reception（歓迎会・受付・電波） The signal reception in the server room is terrible — I had to step outside just to take the call. We're hosting a welcome reception for the new members on Friday. The hotel reception opens at seven in the morning.
# way too much（度を超えて多い） I ordered way too much food at the restaurant. She spent way too much time on that one slide. We've got way too many tabs open right now.
# protagonist（主人公・中心人物） In most RPGs, you build the protagonist's stats from scratch. She's the protagonist of her own story, not a supporting character. The protagonist's motivation drives the entire plot forward.

item.title: 英語学習 listening_20260616
item.description:
# ════════════════════════════════════ # 2026/06/16 英語学習 リスニング教材 # ════════════════════════════════════
# ──────────────────────────────────── # SPEAK MODE: 英語が流暢に話せるようになった # ────────────────────────────────────
I can now speak English fluently — I found a great method that really works. My English has really clicked — I finally figured out what works for me.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # SPEAK MODE: Claude Codeを英語の先生として使っている # ────────────────────────────────────
I use Claude Code as my English teacher — I set up a system prompt so it acts as a language coach. Now I'm confident I can study any language, not just English, but also Chinese or Korean, using Claude Code. I've been using Claude Code as a language tutor — just dropped in a system prompt and it works like a charm. Now I feel like I could tackle any language: Chinese, Korean, you name it.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # SPEAK MODE: ウェルカムパーティー # ────────────────────────────────────
Did you forget about the welcome party yesterday? I was waiting for you and I missed you — but don't worry about missing it, you don't have to pay anything. Anyway, there wasn't enough food, so if you had been there, it would have caused a serious food shortage! Hey, did you forget about yesterday's welcome party? I was looking for you! No worries about missing it though — no charge. Besides, we barely had enough food as it was, so honestly, it's probably a good thing you didn't show up!
# ──────────────────────────────────── # 単語: besides / barely # ────────────────────────────────────
besides barely I don't think we should go there now — besides, it's already closed. The café was just barely big enough for all of us.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # SPEAK MODE: 食料がギリギリだった # ────────────────────────────────────
There wasn't an excessive amount of food — it was just about right, so if you had been there, we might not have had enough! Honestly, the food was just barely enough — if you'd shown up, we would've run out for sure!
# ──────────────────────────────────── # TRANSLATE MODE: 外国人は英語を笑わない # ────────────────────────────────────
Native speakers won't laugh at your English no matter how rough it is — they'll actually try to connect with you, so learning English can be pretty fun. Regardless of how awkward your English may be, native speakers will never mock you — in fact, they tend to engage with you proactively, which makes language learning a genuinely rewarding experience. However clumsy your English is, native speakers won't laugh — if anything, they'll go out of their way to chat with you, which is honestly what makes learning a language so much fun.
# ここがポイントです。 # Casual は「try to connect」、Professional は「engage proactively」、Super Native は「go out of their way to chat」と、同じ意味をレベルごとに異なる語彙で表現しています。
However clumsy your English is, native speakers won't laugh — if anything, they'll go out of their way to chat with you.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # 単語・フレーズ: if anything / go out of their way # ────────────────────────────────────
if anything go out of their way The new café isn't worse than before — if anything, it's even better. She went out of her way to make sure everyone felt welcome at the party.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # SPEAK MODE: コーヒーで眠れない # ────────────────────────────────────
I didn't know that until now. Oh, I had no idea!
# ──────────────────────────────────── # TRANSLATE MODE: 朝のアラームシステム # ────────────────────────────────────
I always set an alarm before I sleep — no way I'm trusting myself to wake up naturally. And it's not just any alarm; it's one that won't stop unless you scan a QR code. I've set that QR code to a bike-share station about a five-minute walk from my place, so I'm forced to go outside and take a little walk just to turn it off. That's my system for actually waking up. I make it a rule to always set an alarm before sleeping, as I've learned I simply cannot rely on waking up naturally. The alarm I use isn't a conventional one — it requires scanning a QR code to be dismissed. I've assigned that QR code to a bike-share station roughly five minutes on foot from my apartment, which means I'm obligated to step outside and take a short walk every morning just to silence it. It's a system I've designed to guarantee I actually get up. I never sleep without setting an alarm — trusting myself to wake up on my own is just not happening. On top of that, I use this alarm that won't shut off unless you scan a QR code. And I've linked that QR code to a bike-share station about a five-minute walk away, so to kill the alarm I literally have to drag myself outside for a little walk. That's the whole trick — by the time I'm back, I'm actually awake.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # TRANSLATE MODE: コーヒーで眠れない # ────────────────────────────────────
I had coffee, so now I can't sleep at all… I actually want to sleep though. I made the mistake of having coffee, so now I'm not feeling sleepy at all — which is rather frustrating given that I actually want to get some rest. I made the mistake of having coffee and now I'm wide awake… of all the times to not be able to sleep.
# ここがポイントです。 # 「コーヒーを飲んでしまった後悔」の表し方として、Casual は単純な because 接続、Super Native は「I made the mistake of ~ing」という構文を使っています。後悔のニュアンスをより強く出したいときは後者が自然です。
I made the mistake of having coffee and now I'm wide awake… of all the times to not be able to sleep.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # 単語・構文: of all the times / wide awake / I made the mistake of ~ing # ────────────────────────────────────
of all the times to forget your umbrella… of all the days to run into your ex… I made the mistake of skipping breakfast and now I can't focus at all. I was wide awake at 3 AM and had no idea why.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # SPEAK MODE: よりによってみすぼらしい服の日に元カノに会う # ────────────────────────────────────
Of all the times to be wearing my worst outfit… and I run into my ex. Of all the days to look like a complete mess — of course I'd run into my ex.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # 単語: run into # ────────────────────────────────────
run into I ran into my old university professor at the gym — hadn't seen him in like three years. I ran into a few unexpected problems while setting up the new system. We ran into each other at the supermarket completely by chance.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # ポッドキャスト: wishful thinking / be guilty of / a kind way of saying # ────────────────────────────────────
wishful thinking I absolutely have hope for peace in the world. Thinking the project will finish on time without any delays is just wishful thinking. I'm guilty of checking my phone first thing every morning. We're all guilty of that. Saying someone's idea is "interesting" is sometimes just a kind way of saying you don't agree with it. That's optimistic — which is a kind way of saying it's unlikely.
# ここがポイントです。 # 「That's optimistic」は表面上は褒め言葉ですが、文脈によって「まずないと思う」という婉曲的な否定になります。直接否定を避ける英語の定番テクニックです。
That's optimistic — which is a kind way of saying it's unlikely.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # 単語: skeptic / convinced # ────────────────────────────────────
skeptic skeptical A lot of skeptics said electric cars would never catch on — that's clearly changed. I'm not convinced this approach will work at scale. I'm convinced we need to rethink the whole strategy before moving forward.
# ここがポイントです。 # skeptical は「証拠がないから信じない」という論理ベースの態度、cynical は「どうせダメだろう」という感情ベースの否定的態度を指します。
I'm skeptical about the new policy, but I'm willing to give it a chance.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # ポッドキャスト: a lot of times / you might say / Never have I done that # ────────────────────────────────────
a lot of times A lot of times, the best solution is the simplest one. You might say it's risky, but I think it's worth trying. A lot of times you might say, we talked about this — but is that just wishful thinking? Never have I done that in my life. Never have I felt so out of place.
# ここがポイントです。 # 否定副詞（never・rarely・not once）を文頭に置くと主語と助動詞が倒置します。通常形と倒置形の違いを確認してください。
I have never done that in my life. Never have I done that in my life.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # 単語: it's just not a thing / not my thing / clearance racks # ────────────────────────────────────
it's just not a thing it's not my thing that's not really a thing here I've never been into karaoke — it's just not my thing. I love digging through clearance racks — you can find amazing stuff for almost nothing. I found this jacket on the clearance rack for ten dollars.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # 単語: burst someone's bubble / brand new / in advance / by the time # ────────────────────────────────────
burst someone's bubble I hate to burst your bubble, but the deadline was moved up to next Monday. Sorry to burst your bubble, but that feature was already built by another team. brand new This laptop is brand new — straight out of the box. That café is brand new to me, even though it's been open for years. in advance Please let me know in advance if you can't make it. We recorded this a bit in advance, so things may have changed by now. by the time By the time I got to the station, the train had already left. By the time you finish reading this, the update will have rolled out.
# ここがポイントです。 # 「by the time」は未来完了形（will have done）と組み合わせることが多く、「その時点ですでに〜している」という時間のズレを表現します。
By the time you hear this, it will have been out for a while.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # 構文: it will have been out a while / transatlantic / charter / last minute / was able to # ────────────────────────────────────
It will have been out a while by the time you hear this. Most of you have probably already seen it. It would do transatlantic flights. It would charter a plane and then sell any extra tickets really cheap last minute. I was able to fly to Brussels from Salt Lake City for thirty-five dollars.
# ここがポイントです。 # 「would do / would charter」は過去の繰り返し習慣を表します。状態動詞（exist など）には使えず、その場合は「used to」を使います。
It used to exist, but I don't think it does anymore. It would charter a plane and sell last-minute tickets at a huge discount.
# ここがポイントです。 # 「could fly」と「was able to fly」の違いに注意してください。過去に実際に達成できた一回限りの成功には「was able to」が正確です。
I was able to fly to Brussels for just thirty-five dollars.

item.title: 英語学習 listening_20260617 (1)
item.description:
# ==================================================== # Listening Material — 2026/06/17 English Session # ====================================================
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # お礼・共感表現 # ────────────────────────────────────────
Thanks for opening up about something so personal yesterday. I'm really hoping your brother gets better soon.
Thank you for trusting me with something so personal yesterday. I sincerely hope your brother makes a full recovery.
I really appreciate you sharing something that personal with me yesterday. I'm keeping your brother in my thoughts and wishing him a speedy recovery.
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # 未来完了形・確定した未来の予定 # ────────────────────────────────────────
I will have finished the task by the time you come. I'll have the task done by the time you get here.
I'm leaving for India in three months and staying for a month.
The deployment will go live in two hours. The server crashed after two hours of running the script.
ここがポイントです。 「in+期間」は今この瞬間が基準点のカウントダウン、「after+期間」は文中の別の出来事が基準点です。
I'm leaving for India in three months.
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # 現在進行形による確定未来 vs will vs be going to # ────────────────────────────────────────
I'm leaving for India in three months and staying for a month. I'm going to visit India next year. I think I'll go to India someday.
ここがポイントです。 be+ingは航空券・予約など手配済みの確定予定、be going toは意図・計画レベル、willはその場の判断・予測です。
I'm leaving for India in three months and staying for a month.
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # 過去の習慣（used to / would） # ────────────────────────────────────────
I used to play soccer when I was young, but now I can't because of my right knee injury. I used to play soccer all the time growing up, but my right knee gave out on me so I had to stop.
I used to play badminton all the time growing up, but my left foot injury took me out of it. I used to play badminton all the time growing up, but a left foot injury forced me to quit.
ここがポイントです。 「used to」は今はしていない過去の習慣の対比を強調します。「would」も過去の習慣を表せますが、「but now」と組み合わせると対比のニュアンスが弱くなります。
I used to play badminton all the time growing up, but a left foot injury forced me to quit.
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # have no option but to / no choice but to # ────────────────────────────────────────
I have no option but to quit the task. I have no choice but to drop the task.
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # look like she's getting / seem to be # ────────────────────────────────────────
She looks like she's getting tired. She seems to be running out of steam.
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # 倒置構文 vs 通常語順 # ────────────────────────────────────────
Never have I committed a minor offense. I've never committed even the slightest offense.
ここがポイントです。 否定副詞を文頭に置いた倒置構文は文語的・強調的でドラマチックに聞こえます。日常会話では通常語順のほうが自然です。
I've never committed even the slightest offense.
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # 先生の車に小石（過去の記憶・慣用表現） # ────────────────────────────────────────
Oh wait, actually one thing comes to mind. Now that I think about it, I might've thrown a pebble at my teacher's car when I was a kid.
Oh wait, actually there is one thing I just remembered. Now that I think about it, I may have thrown a small stone at my teacher's car when I was young.
Oh hold on, actually something just came back to me. Now that I think about it, I think I might've chucked a pebble at my teacher's car back when I was a kid.
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # Now that I've done my best / have no regrets # ────────────────────────────────────────
Now that I've done my best, I have no regrets. Now that I've given it everything I've got, I have zero regrets.
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # during / while / when の使い分け # ────────────────────────────────────────
We found three critical bugs during the code review. The model kept hallucinating while I was fine-tuning the parameters. The server crashed when the load exceeded the threshold.
ここがポイントです。 duringは名詞で期間を範囲指定、whileはS+Vで2つの動作の同時進行、whenはS+Vで時間軸上の点やきっかけを表します。
We found three critical bugs during the code review.
I only study the flashcards which contain new vocabulary. The script generates a log file, which gets uploaded to S3 automatically.
ここがポイントです。 カンマなしのwhichは先行詞を限定するフィルター、カンマありのwhichは「and it」に近い補足情報の追加です。
The script generates a log file, which gets uploaded to S3 automatically.
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # All Ears English Podcast イントロ # ────────────────────────────────────────
Welcome to the All Ears English Podcast, downloaded more than 200 million times. Are you feeling stuck with your English? We'll show you how to become fearless and fluent by focusing on connection, not perfection.
How do you know if a verb is transitive or intransitive? And more importantly, how can this affect connection? Learn all of this and more today.
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # 他動詞・自動詞・両用動詞 # ────────────────────────────────────────
I'll email you the report. The server crashed at midnight.
"Run" is ambitransitive. I run a company. I run every day.
ここがポイントです。 他動詞は後ろに目的語が必須、自動詞は不要、両用動詞はどちらの用法も持ちます。判定は「主語＝補語が成立するか」で機械的に見分けられます。
I run a company. I run every day.
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # 使役構文（have + O + 原形） # ────────────────────────────────────────
I'll have the script generate a report automatically. We had the AI summarize the meeting notes. I have ChatGPT compile everything into a CSV, which I dump straight into Anki.
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # Linking Verb（連結動詞） # ────────────────────────────────────────
It starts acting as my English teacher. The server becomes unresponsive under heavy load. The build process gets stuck at the linting stage. The API started returning 500 errors after deployment. She seems happy. He remained calm.
ここがポイントです。 be動詞はLinking Verbの代表格ですが、become / get / start / turn / seem / remain も同じグループで、主語の状態を後ろの補語にイコールで結びます。
It starts acting as my English teacher.
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # 非制限用法 which（カンマ付き） # ────────────────────────────────────────
I have it compile everything into a CSV, which I dump straight into Anki to turn into flashcards. The script generates a log file, which gets uploaded to S3 automatically. We only deploy the services which pass all the CI checks.
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # 所有格 his vs 目的格 him # ────────────────────────────────────────
I put his gentle leader on so he wouldn't pull too hard. I put his collar on before we headed out for the morning walk. I put him in his crate.
ここがポイントです。 動作の直接の対象が人そのものなら目的格（him）、人が所有する物なら所有格（his）を使います。
I put his gentle leader on so he wouldn't pull too hard.
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # ChatGPT + Anki 英語学習法 # ────────────────────────────────────────
My strategy for acquiring English is to use ChatGPT and an app called Anki. I set up a system prompt for ChatGPT so that it starts acting as my English teacher.
In my daily life, when I want to express my feelings in English but don't know how to say it, I send the Japanese word to the chat, and it sends back the translated version. Or when I can't understand an English word, I send it over, and ChatGPT also sends back the Japanese meaning along with the IPA.
At the end of the day, I have ChatGPT generate a CSV file containing every word or phrase I used that day. I then import this file into Anki and turn it into flashcards to study.
My go-to method for picking up English is combining ChatGPT with Anki. I've got ChatGPT set up with a custom prompt so it acts like a personal English tutor. Whenever I'm not sure how to phrase something, I just type it in Japanese and get the English version back instantly. Same goes for words I don't recognize; I throw them at ChatGPT and get the meaning plus IPA right away. Then at night, I have it compile everything into a CSV, which I dump straight into Anki to turn into flashcards.
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # 英語＝体育論（PE比喩） # ────────────────────────────────────────
I would just say that English is more like PE than math, in the sense that it needs daily practice. What I mean is, math or physics can sometimes improve all at once, but PE doesn't work that way. Your muscles need time to repair, and that process takes two or three days. This shows that you can't get stronger overnight.
What I'm trying to say is that English is more like a sport than a subject like math. It demands daily reps, not occasional cramming. With math or physics, things can click all at once. But with something physical, your muscles literally need two or three days to repair and rebuild. You just can't rush that kind of strength.
# ──────────────────────────────────────── # 単語・イディオム・フレーズ例文集 # ────────────────────────────────────────
I've been so forgetful lately — I left my umbrella on the train again.
This time there are higher stakes, since the client is watching the demo live. His reputation is at stake if this product launch fails. We need to align with all the stakeholders before the release.
The vet recommended a muzzle to keep him calm during the visit. Before our walk, I put his gentle leader on so he wouldn't pull too hard.
Her story about overcoming failure really resonated with the audience. Seeing my dog wag his tail when I get home always makes my day.
I'm a curious mind by nature, so I end up reading about random topics I know nothing about.
If we could automate this whole process, it'd be fantastic.
This rule applies to everyone, with no exception.
It's fascinating how the human brain can rewire itself after an injury.
If you found this tutorial helpful, hit subscribe on our channel so you don't miss the next one.
Constantly switching frameworks without mastering the fundamentals will stunt your progress as a developer.
As long as the tests pass, you can merge the pull request.
After years of experience, you develop an intuition for spotting mistakes.
Instead of overthinking grammar rules, just dive in with the language and start speaking.
Using "do" instead of "make" in certain phrases is a glaring error that sounds really off to native speakers.
The migration will take roughly two weeks to complete. Sales increased by about 15%.
"Give it a shot" is a more idiomatic way of saying "try it."
In the end, it all comes down to how well you communicate with your team.
We used a ton of those terms today.
Do you need help with the database setup or anything else? Let me know if you need this fixed or anything else.
This approach is simpler in the sense that it requires fewer dependencies.
Now that I think about it, I might've thrown a pebble at my teacher's car when I was a kid.
I'm convinced he's going to be a great basketball player.
I'm heading to India in three months.
Then we're all screwed. We're so screwed.

item.title: 英語学習 listening_20260617
item.description:
# ════════════════════════════════════ # Listening Material — 2026/06/17 # ════════════════════════════════════
# ──────────────────────────────────── # 挨拶・導入 # ────────────────────────────────────
Good morning! Good morning. I hope you're doing well. Mornin'! I'm doing well. Excited to be recording with you guys.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # ポッドキャスト・収録文脈 # ────────────────────────────────────
Aubrey and I are gonna have more time on the mic together while Lindsay is out. But you will still be hearing her voice, don't you worry. We're super pumped to be recording this episode together. Haruto and I are holding it down while our co-host is out. But don't worry, you'll still get your regular content.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # 英語学習・スタート宣言 # ────────────────────────────────────
My English learning journey has just begun. I'm just getting started with English.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # get + 過去分詞：状態変化の表現 # ────────────────────────────────────
She is tired. She is getting tired. She got tired.
ここがポイントです。 最初の文は「すでに疲れている状態」、二番目は「だんだん疲れてきている変化の途中」、三番目は「疲れた（変化の完了）」を表しています。
She is getting tired.
I think she's getting tired now. I think she's starting to burn out.
I'm getting started. I got excited. I got confused. I got involved. I got promoted.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # 友人・欠席・お兄さんの死の文脈 # ────────────────────────────────────
My friend didn't show up at the welcome party. Earlier today I ran into him. I asked him if he had forgotten the party. I was never mad at him. I just teased him a little. I really care about him. He must be devastated right now. He must be in a really bad mental state.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # 共感・気遣いの表現 # ────────────────────────────────────
I'm keeping your brother in my thoughts. I really hope your brother regains consciousness soon. Losing a sibling is something unlike anything else. Just being there, even in silence, can mean everything.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # 聞き返せなかった・怖かった文脈 # ────────────────────────────────────
I didn't understand the phrase "pass out" that you used. I couldn't bring myself to ask because you looked so serious. I was afraid it meant something like death or something really critical. But later someone explained it to me, and now I understand. I'm so sorry. I really hope your brother regains consciousness soon.
I was too scared to ask. I thought it might mean something really serious, like dying. I'm really glad I know now, and I'm keeping your brother in my thoughts.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # couldn't bring myself to / bring myself to # ────────────────────────────────────
I couldn't bring myself to ask because he looked so serious. I couldn't bring myself to tell her the truth. I finally brought myself to call him back.
ここがポイントです。 couldn't bring myself to は「物理的にできなかった」のではなく「心理的に踏み出せなかった」というニュアンスを持ちます。
I couldn't bring myself to ask.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # 調べる系の動詞：look up / examine / investigate # ────────────────────────────────────
I looked it up. I went and looked it up. I checked the details before replying. The doctor examined the patient carefully. The QA team needs to examine the logs carefully before we push the fix to production. The investigators are looking into the cause of the accident.
ここがポイントです。 look up はサッと調べる、examine は体系的・詳細に調べる、investigate は問題や事件を掘り下げて調査するという強さの違いがあります。
I went and looked it up.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # pass out：2つの意味 # ────────────────────────────────────
He nearly passed out after standing in the heat for three hours. She passed out from exhaustion after the long flight. Can you pass out the handouts before the meeting starts? Can you pass out the sprint review documents before the meeting starts?
ここがポイントです。 pass out には「気を失う」と「配る」の二つの意味があります。文脈で判断することが重要です。
He nearly passed out from exhaustion.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # ポッドキャスト会話から：相槌・同意表現 # ────────────────────────────────────
Exactly. Exactly. But let's go through these. We used a bunch here in this roleplay. Right. Totally. That makes sense. Fair enough. I hear you.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # go through / a bunch：頻出句動詞・表現 # ────────────────────────────────────
Let's go through these one by one. We went through a lot of material today. Let's go through the notes before we wrap up. We used a bunch of new expressions in this session. There were a bunch of things I didn't know before. I've got a bunch of questions for you.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # SPEAK MODE まとめ：自然な言い換え表現 # ────────────────────────────────────
I'm just getting started with English. I think she's getting tired now. I think she's starting to burn out. I looked it up. I went and looked it up. I was too scared to ask. I couldn't bring myself to ask. I'm keeping your brother in my thoughts. I really hope your brother regains consciousness soon.

item.title: 英語学習 listening_20260618
item.description:
# ════════════════════════════════════ # リスニング教材 2026/06/18 # ════════════════════════════════════
# ──────────────────────────────────── # click # ────────────────────────────────────
After months of confusion, the concept finally clicked for me. Things clicked between us the moment we started talking. It just clicked — I finally understood what she meant.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # in the sense (that) # ────────────────────────────────────
It's done, in the sense that it compiles, but there's still a lot of cleanup left. He's a good friend, in the sense that he always shows up when it matters. It was a success, in the sense that nobody got hurt.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # occasional cramming # ────────────────────────────────────
I don't study every day — it's more like occasional cramming right before exams. Occasional cramming might get you through the test, but it won't stick long-term.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # all at once # ────────────────────────────────────
All at once, the power went out and the whole room went dark. Don't try to do everything all at once — take it one step at a time. All at once, everyone started laughing.
ここがポイントです。 all at once には「突然」と「同時に」の2つの意味があります。文脈で使い分けましょう。
All at once, the lights went out. He tried to carry all the bags all at once.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # back-to-back # ────────────────────────────────────
Could the Knicks go back-to-back? They won the championship back-to-back. We had three meetings back-to-back this morning — I'm exhausted.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # colours / with flying colours / true colours # ────────────────────────────────────
She passed the exam with flying colours. He finished the race with flying colours. Don't judge too quickly — wait until you see someone's true colours. That situation really showed his true colours.
ここがポイントです。 with flying colours は「大成功で、見事に」、show one's true colours は「本性を現す」という意味の慣用句です。colours はイギリス英語のつづりで、アメリカ英語では colors となります。
She passed the interview with flying colours.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # nuisance # ────────────────────────────────────
Sorry to be a nuisance, but could you turn the music down a bit? The roadwork outside has been a real nuisance all week. He's not dangerous — just a bit of a nuisance.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # Thanks to ~ （皮肉的な用法） # ────────────────────────────────────
Thanks to you, we missed the train. Thanks to the rain, the outdoor event was ruined. Thanks to his advice, everything went perfectly.
ここがポイントです。 Thanks to は本来ポジティブな「おかげで」ですが、文脈によって「せいで」という皮肉にもなります。引用符で属性を囲む使い方は英語で疑念や皮肉を示す典型的な手法です。
# ──────────────────────────────────── # All you have to do is (just) ~ # ────────────────────────────────────
All you have to do is just ask. All you have to do is show up on time. All you have to do is click the button and it's done.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # I've given it everything I've got # ────────────────────────────────────
I've given it everything I've got, so I have no regrets now. She gave the performance everything she had. He left it all on the field — gave it everything he'd got.
ここがポイントです。 I've given it everything I've got は「全力を出し切った」という過去から現在への結果を表す現在完了形です。今まさに頑張っている最中を表す I'm throwing myself into it とはニュアンスが異なります。
I'm throwing myself into studying English right now. I've given it everything I've got, so I have no regrets.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # SPEAK MODE — 全力を注いでいる # ────────────────────────────────────
I'm putting all my energy into studying English right now. I'm throwing myself into studying English right now.
ここがポイントです。 put all one's energy into は「〜に全エネルギーを注ぐ」という定番の言い方です。throw oneself into はさらに熱意と没頭感が加わった表現です。
I'm throwing myself into studying English right now.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # SPEAK MODE — この方法を続ければ # ────────────────────────────────────
If I stick with this method, I'll be a good English speaker in no time. Keep this up, and I'll be fluent before I know it. If I stick with this, I'll get there eventually.
ここがポイントです。 stick with は「〜を続ける・貫く」、in no time は「すぐに・あっという間に」、before I know it は「気づいたら・いつの間にか」という口語表現です。
Keep this up, and I'll be fluent before I know it.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # 条件文の4パターン # ────────────────────────────────────
# ゼロ条件文（一般的事実） If you heat ice, it melts. If you mix red and blue, you get purple. If it rains, the ground gets wet.
# 第1条件文（現実的に起こりうる未来） If I keep this up, I'll be a good speaker soon. If the weather is nice, we'll go for a walk. If you study hard, you'll pass the exam.
# 第2条件文（非現実的な仮定・可能性が低い） If I were you, I would study harder. If I had more time, I'd travel the world. If she knew the answer, she would tell us.
ここがポイントです。 第1条件文と第2条件文の違いは文法ルールではなく、話者の意識です。「本当に起こると思っている」なら第1、「仮の話・可能性が低い」と感じているなら第2を選びます。
If I keep this up, I'll be fluent. If I kept this up, I'd be fluent.
# 第3条件文（過去の事実と反対の仮定） If I had studied harder, I would have passed. If she had left earlier, she wouldn't have missed the train. If we had known about it, we would have come.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # I'm telling you # ────────────────────────────────────
I'm telling you, that place has the best coffee I've ever had. I'm telling you, this is going to change everything. I'm telling you, you'd love it there.
# ──────────────────────────────────── # older = faster / younger = better in the end # ────────────────────────────────────
Adults can often learn grammar rules faster than children. Children tend to reach native-like pronunciation more naturally. It's not that adults can't learn — it's that the process looks different.

item.title: 英語学習 listening_20260623 (1)
item.description:
# 赤羽・試験の予定を伝える
Today I'm going to Akabane to take an exam for a job. I'm heading to Akabane today for a job exam.
# be -ing で未来の予定を表す
I'm going to Akabane today. I'm going to Akabane right now.
ここがポイントです。 前者は「今日行く予定がある」という確定した未来を表し、後者は「今まさに向かっている最中」という意味になります。時間を示す語（today / right now）が文脈を決定的にします。
I'm going to Akabane today.
# be going to との使い分け
I'm going to Akabane today. I'm going to go to Akabane today. I will go to Akabane today.
ここがポイントです。 be -ingは個人的にすでに決めている確定予定、be going toはやや説明的な計画、willはその場で決めた・単純な未来予測に近い響きになります。
I'm going to Akabane today.
# メールに返信する
I need to reply to an email from Marugata. I've gotta get back to Marugata's email.
# ToDoリストで主語を省略するスタイル
Reply to Marugata's email. Replying to Marugata's email.
# reply to の前置詞
I need to reply to an email from Marugata.
# end up -ing
I always end up replying to emails late at night. I ended up staying up way too late last night. I ended up spending more than I planned.
# right の強調副詞用法
I usually start packing right before my trip, not days in advance. I don't study every day — it's more like occasional cramming right before exams. He called me right after the meeting.
# keep it up と stay up の違い
I couldn't keep it up after the first week. I couldn't stay up that late.
ここがポイントです。 keep it upは「努力・習慣・ペースを維持し続ける」、stay upは「夜更かしする・起きていられる」という意味で、まったく別の表現です。
I tried to exercise every morning, but I couldn't keep it up after the first week.
I wanted to finish the show, but I couldn't stay up that late.
# without exception
Everyone in the class passed the test, without exception. The rule applies to everyone, without exception. All flights were delayed, without exception.
# on campus
I really wanna get some sleep, but there's no place to sleep on campus. There seems to be no place to sleep on campus. I really need to crash somewhere, but there's nowhere to sleep on campus.
# in campus と on campus の違い
There's no place to sleep on campus.
ここがポイントです。 大学の敷地内を表すときは慣用的に前置詞 on を使います。in ではありません。
There's no place to sleep on campus.
# There seems to be
There seems to be no place to sleep on campus. There seems to be a problem with the reservation. There seems to be a misunderstanding between us.
# get over / break up
It took her a few months to get over the breakup. They decided to break up after dating for two years. He's still not over his ex. It's hard to get over something like that. They broke up out of nowhere.

item.title: 英語学習 listening_20260623 (2)
item.description:
# ════════════════════════════════ # Listening Material — 2026/06/23 # ════════════════════════════════
# ── 人の態度を表す表現 ──
His attitude toward me is really harsh. I think he might hate me. His attitude toward me is really harsh. I think he might hate me.
He's been pretty cold towards me lately. I think he might hate me. He's been pretty cold towards me lately — I think he might hate me.
ここがポイントです。 前者は今まさに感じている態度のきつさ、後者は「最近になって変わった」という時間的変化のニュアンスがあります。
He's been pretty cold towards me lately — I think he might hate me.
# ── severe vs harsh ──
His attitude is really harsh. His attitude is pretty cold. His attitude is quite rough.
ここがポイントです。 severe は天候・病状・罰則など「深刻な厳しさ」に使います。人の態度・口調のきつさには harsh、cold、rough を使いましょう。
His attitude is really harsh.
# ── may vs might（主観的推測） ──
It may rain tomorrow. He might hate me.
ここがポイントです。 may は客観的・公式的な可能性の表現、might は「もしかして〜かも」という個人の主観的な推測・不安に使います。口語では might の使用頻度が圧倒的に高いです。
He might hate me.
# ── make the mistake of -ing ──
I made the mistake of trusting him too much. I made the mistake of telling her the truth. I made the mistake of arriving too early.
I shouldn't have trusted him so much. I shouldn't have told her that. I shouldn't have gone there alone.
ここがポイントです。 make the mistake of -ing は「〜するというミスを犯す」という構文。口語の後悔表現では shouldn't have + 過去分詞 の方が頻度が高く、より自然に聞こえます。
I made the mistake of trusting him too much. I shouldn't have trusted him so much.
# ── The same goes for / That applies to ──
The same goes for trusting his promises. The same goes for his sister. The same goes for everything else he said.
That applies to trusting his promises too. That applies to everyone in the team. That applies to all of us.
ここがポイントです。 The same goes for は口語でカジュアルに「同じことが〜にも当てはまる」と言いたい時に使います。That applies to はやや硬めで、説明的な会話やビジネスシーンにも使いやすい表現です。
The same goes for trusting his promises.
# ── 英語の主語省略について ──
I made the mistake of trusting him. I should have known better. I can't believe I trusted him.
# 英語では命令文を除き、主語を省略することはできません。 # 日本語の感覚で主語を落とすと文として成立しないので注意が必要です。
# ── このセッションの主要表現まとめ ──
His attitude toward me is really harsh. I think he might hate me. He's been pretty cold towards me lately — I think he might hate me. I made the mistake of trusting him too much. I shouldn't have trusted him so much. The same goes for trusting his promises. That applies to trusting his promises too.

item.title: 英語学習 listening_20260623
item.description:
# ════════════════════════════════════ # リスニング教材 2026/06/23 # ════════════════════════════════════
# ────────────────────────────────── # 初恋との再会：連絡を取って会いに行った # ──────────────────────────────────
I had reached out to her a few days before, and yesterday we finally met up. After we got back in touch, I went to see her yesterday.
# ────────────────────────────────── # 初恋との再会：描写 # ──────────────────────────────────
She's even more beautiful than when we were young. We went to the same elementary school together. Ten years after that first crush, we finally had drinks, just the two of us, and it turned out to be a really wonderful night. I ended up spending the evening with my first crush, and honestly? It was kind of magical.
# ────────────────────────────────── # 時制：過去完了と過去の使い分け # ──────────────────────────────────
I had reached out to her a few days before, and yesterday we finally met up. After we got back in touch, I went to see her yesterday.
ここがポイントです。 前者は「会った」より前の出来事を過去完了で明示したパターン。後者は after という接続詞で時系列が明らかなため、過去完了なしでも成立します。
I had reached out to her a few days before, and yesterday we finally met up.
# ────────────────────────────────── # 気遣いの表現：深刻な状況で相手の安否を尋ねる # ──────────────────────────────────
I've been thinking about you and your brother… how is he? I don't want to pry, but… I've been thinking about your brother. I hope he's okay. I've been keeping you both in my thoughts. Please don't feel like you have to update me — but I'm here if you want to talk.
# ────────────────────────────────── # 気遣いの表現：How's ~ doing との違い # ──────────────────────────────────
How's your brother doing? I've been thinking about you and your brother… how is he?
ここがポイントです。 前者は回復を前提としたニュアンスが含まれ、深刻な状況では軽すぎる場合があります。後者は結果を決めつけず、相手に委ねた聞き方です。
I've been thinking about you and your brother… how is he?
# ────────────────────────────────── # 弔慰：言葉がないときの表現 # ──────────────────────────────────
I have no words. I really care about you. I'm really worried about how you're holding up. I have no words. I'm just really glad you have people around you, and I'm here too. I have no words. I'm here if you want to talk. I'm here if you need me. I'm so sorry.
# ────────────────────────────────── # 冗談：英語が上手くなったら性格が悪くなるかも # ──────────────────────────────────
What if I get super fluent and start saying all kinds of awful stuff in English — would you actually prefer the version of me who couldn't speak it? Hypothetically, if I became perfectly fluent and started saying terrible things — would you find yourself missing the version of me who couldn't communicate at all? What if I get crazy fluent and just turn into a total jerk about it — like, would you actually miss the old me who could barely say anything?
# ────────────────────────────────── # 英語脳が育ってきた実感 # ──────────────────────────────────
Ever since I started learning this way, I can feel my English brain developing really fast. Since adopting this approach, I've noticed my English intuition growing at a remarkable pace. Honestly, since I started doing it this way, it's like something just clicked — I actually feel myself thinking in English now.
# ────────────────────────────────── # 言いたいことが出るまでの初速が速くなった # ──────────────────────────────────
I feel like the words are coming to me faster now — like, the time between thinking and speaking has gotten way shorter. I've noticed that the words come to me much more quickly now — the lag between thought and expression has noticeably decreased. It's like the gap between wanting to say something and actually saying it has just… shrunk. Things are coming out way faster now.
# ────────────────────────────────── # 受験英語と話す英語は別物 # ──────────────────────────────────
And honestly, you don't really need fancy grammar or vocab to just speak English. I've been realizing lately that no matter how well you can read hard texts, like acing university entrance exams, it doesn't mean you can actually talk. What's become increasingly clear to me is that fluency in speaking requires an entirely different skill set from academic English proficiency. Scoring well on entrance exams and actually being able to hold a conversation are simply two different things. And like… you don't need to be some grammar genius to speak English. I've really been feeling lately that all that exam-prep stuff — reading dense passages, memorizing obscure vocab — none of that actually makes you a speaker.
# ────────────────────────────────── # 確信・念押しの表現：I'm telling you # ──────────────────────────────────
I'm telling you, you'd love it. I'm telling you, that place has the best coffee I've ever had. Trust me, you'd be obsessed with Jiro-style ramen.
# ────────────────────────────────── # 勧める表現：二郎系ラーメン # ──────────────────────────────────
I'm telling you, you'd love it. And I'm sure you'd be into Jiro-style ramen too. Trust me, you'd be obsessed with Jiro-style ramen.
# ────────────────────────────────── # 強い意志・自信の表現 # ──────────────────────────────────
I'm confident I'll be a solid English speaker within a year. I'm determined to become a fluent English speaker within a year. I genuinely think I'll be speaking English fluently within a year. I can already feel it happening.
# ────────────────────────────────── # confident vs convinced の違い # ──────────────────────────────────
I'm confident I'll be a solid English speaker within a year. I'm convinced that the new policy will work.
ここがポイントです。 前者は自分自身の内側から来る自信。後者は外部の証拠や状況によって確信させられたニュアンスです。自分の意志・能力への自信には confident を使います。
I'm confident I'll be a solid English speaker within a year.
# ────────────────────────────────── # reach out / get back in touch # ──────────────────────────────────
I reached out to her after all these years. After we got back in touch, I went to see her yesterday. I had reached out to her a few days before, and yesterday we finally met up.

item.title: 英語学習 listening_20260625
item.description:
# ======================================== # Listening Material — 2026/06/25 # ========================================
# ---------------------------------------- # 誕生日・年齢表現 # ----------------------------------------
Happy birthday, Farhan! You're 25 now, right? Happy birthday, Farhan! Just turned 25, huh? Nice to meet you. She just turned 30 last week. He turned 25 in April.
ここがポイントです。 年齢の確認には become ではなく turn または be を使います。You become 25 は不自然で、You turned 25 または You're 25 now が正解です。
You're 25 now, right?
# ---------------------------------------- # 単語・フレーズ：savvy / bottling up / syndicate # ----------------------------------------
She's pretty savvy when it comes to finding cheap flights. He's been bottling up his feelings instead of talking to anyone. A syndicate of investors pooled their money to buy the property. She's tech-savvy and always knows the best deals. It's not healthy to bottle up your emotions. The crime syndicate had connections all over the city.
# ---------------------------------------- # 単語・フレーズ：pick up / combine A with B # ----------------------------------------
She picked up English really fast just by watching TV shows every day. He picked up a few phrases while traveling in Spain. Try combining exercise with listening to podcasts — it makes the most of your time. She combined her love of cooking with her passion for travel. You can pick up a lot just by listening to native speakers.
# ---------------------------------------- # 単語・フレーズ：stick with / pebble # ----------------------------------------
It's hard at first, but stick with it and you'll start to see results. Stick with what you know and build from there. She collected a smooth pebble from the beach as a souvenir. The path was lined with small pebbles and wildflowers.
# ---------------------------------------- # throw to vs throw at # ----------------------------------------
Throw the ball to me. She threw confetti at everyone. She threw the gift to him. He threw a pillow at me. The kids were throwing snowballs at each other. Can you throw the keys to me?
ここがポイントです。 throw to は受け取ってもらうために投げる、throw at は的として投げつけるという意図の違いがあります。shout、point、look も同じ使い分けができます。
Throw the ball to me. He threw a pillow at me.
# ---------------------------------------- # Oh hold on / something just came back to me # ----------------------------------------
Oh hold on, I think I left my umbrella on the train. Oh hold on — something just came back to me. Something just came back to me. Didn't you say you were going to Kyoto this weekend? Wait, hang on — I just remembered something. It just hit me that I forgot to call her back.
# ---------------------------------------- # resonate with / through / in # ----------------------------------------
That story really resonated with me. Her voice resonated through the entire hall. The message resonated across generations. His words still resonate in my mind. The speech resonated deeply with everyone in the room.
ここがポイントです。 resonate は主語が人ではなくモノやコトになるのが英語の自然な語順です。I resonated with the speech は不自然で、The speech resonated with me が正しい形です。
The speech resonated with me.
# ---------------------------------------- # reach out to # ----------------------------------------
She reached out to an old friend she hadn't spoken to in years. Feel free to reach out to me anytime. I'll reach out to her tomorrow and see what she thinks. He reached out to several companies before landing the job.
# ---------------------------------------- # meet vs meet up # ----------------------------------------
I met him at a conference last year. Nice to meet you. We met up for coffee after work. Let's meet up this weekend! We met up with some friends after dinner. Let's meet with your boss tomorrow. We're planning to meet up at the park around noon.
ここがポイントです。 meet は初対面・偶然・公式な場でも使えます。meet up は友人と約束して気軽に集まるニュアンスが強く、フォーマルな打ち合わせには meet を使います。
Let's meet up this weekend! Let's meet with your boss tomorrow.
# ---------------------------------------- # swamp / catch up # ----------------------------------------
I'm totally swamped right now — can we catch up later? She's been swamped with work all week. We should catch up sometime — it's been ages. I'm swamped with emails this morning. Let's catch up over lunch next week.
# ---------------------------------------- # has gotten + 形容詞（変化の現在完了） # ----------------------------------------
The time between thinking and speaking has gotten way shorter. My English has gotten way better lately. The weather has gotten colder this week. We've gotten a lot closer since that trip. Things have gotten a lot easier since I started using that app. It's gotten way harder to find a good seat on the weekend.
ここがポイントです。 get + 形容詞は「〜になる」という変化を表し、現在完了 has gotten と組み合わせると「じわじわ変化してきた」というニュアンスになります。become よりも口語的で自然です。
The time between thinking and speaking has gotten way shorter.
# ---------------------------------------- # had too much to drink / too much to + 動詞 # ----------------------------------------
All I remember is that I had way too much to drink. I had too much to eat at the party — I could barely move. She had too much to handle on her own. We ordered way too much food. I've had way too much coffee today. There's too much to do and not enough time.
# ---------------------------------------- # notice O C（知覚動詞構文） # ----------------------------------------
I've noticed my English intuition growing at a remarkable pace. I noticed her smiling when she heard the news. I could feel something crawling on my arm. I heard her singing in the next room. I watched the sun setting over the mountains. She noticed him standing by the door.
# ---------------------------------------- # I'm heading to / head # ----------------------------------------
I'm heading to the station — see you there! I'm heading home now. Let's head back before it gets dark. I'm heading out — do you need anything? She's heading for Osaka on Friday. Just swing by whenever you're free — I'll be here all afternoon.
# ---------------------------------------- # notice vs realize vs find out # ----------------------------------------
I noticed she seemed quiet today. I realized I'd forgotten my wallet. I found out she was moving to London next month. I noticed the lights were still on when I left. I realized I'd been doing it wrong the whole time. I found out about the event through a friend.
# ---------------------------------------- # open up about / to # ----------------------------------------
She finally opened up about her struggles. He opened up to me for the first time. It takes time to open up. She opened up about her feelings after a long silence. He doesn't open up to many people.
# ---------------------------------------- # trust A with B # ----------------------------------------
Thank you for trusting me with something so personal. She trusted him with her secret. They trusted me with the key. I trust you with my life. He trusted her with everything.
# ---------------------------------------- # that（副詞）vs so # ----------------------------------------
I really appreciate you sharing something that personal with me. I didn't know it was that serious. Is it really that far? I didn't think it would be that cold. I didn't realize she was that upset.
ここがポイントです。 副詞の that は「そこまで・あの程度まで」という共有された文脈への指さしを含みます。so が純粋な強調なのに対し、that には「あのこと、わかってるよね」というニュアンスが加わります。
I didn't know it was that serious.
# ---------------------------------------- # I'm just getting started with # ----------------------------------------
I'm just getting started with Japanese cooking. Let's dive straight into it. She jumped straight into the conversation without saying hello. He got straight into the food without waiting for everyone. I'm just getting started — there's still a lot to learn. We're just getting started with the project.
ここがポイントです。 〇〇 has just begun は物事を客観的に述べる文語的な表現です。I'm just getting started は自分が主体で、やる気やエネルギーが感じられる口語的な表現です。
I'm just getting started.
# ---------------------------------------- # SPEAK MODE：I've been forgetful lately # ----------------------------------------
I've been forgetful lately — or maybe I just am by nature. I don't know if I've just been forgetful lately or if it's always been this way. I've always been a bit absent-minded. I keep forgetting where I put my keys.
# ---------------------------------------- # 気づき表現まとめ # ----------------------------------------
What has become increasingly clear to me is that practice really does make a difference. It turned out that the restaurant was fully booked. I noticed that she'd been quieter than usual. I've come to realize that consistency matters more than intensity. It dawned on me that I hadn't called her in months. I've started to see that small habits add up over time. It hit me that I'd been doing it wrong the whole time. It occurred to me that I never asked how she was feeling.
# ---------------------------------------- # 丸亀うどん・待ち合わせ表現 # ----------------------------------------
So are we still on for Marugame Udon today? I wanted to check in — are we still planning to go to Marugame Udon today? Hey, we still doing Marugame Udon today or what? Are we still on for Marugame today? We still going to Marugame today? How does 6 PM sound? How about going at 6 PM? No worries if something came up — we can always do it another time. Please don't feel obligated — if today doesn't work for you, we can always reschedule. Totally fine if something came up — no pressure, we'll just do it another time. No worries if something came up, but if you're free, let's go today! No worries if something came up — but if today still works for you, I'd love to go! No worries if something came up, but if you're good, let's still do it today!
# ---------------------------------------- # ever since vs since # ----------------------------------------
I've been busy since Monday. We met in Kyoto, and I've been in love with the city ever since. I tried coffee in 2020 and I've loved it ever since. She moved to Tokyo three years ago and has been thriving ever since. I've been hooked ever since. I haven't seen him since the party. Things have been different ever since that conversation.
ここがポイントです。 since は起点を示すだけですが、ever since はあの出来事を境に今日までずっとという継続の強調と転換点としての重みが加わります。文末に単体で置く ever since は特にネイティブらしい使い方です。
I've been hooked ever since.
# ---------------------------------------- # iQOS / unless / out of luck # ----------------------------------------
I left my iQOS with a friend, so I can't smoke unless he shows up. I've left my iQOS with a friend, so I won't be able to use it unless he comes. My iQOS is with a friend, so I'm out of luck until he shows. The last train already left — I'm out of luck. I can't get in unless someone lets me through. We're out of luck if the store is already closed.
# ---------------------------------------- # prior engagement / previous # ----------------------------------------
I'm sorry, I have a prior engagement that evening. She had to decline due to a prior engagement. I already have plans that night. Sorry, I can't make it — I have other commitments.
# ---------------------------------------- # dump into # ----------------------------------------
Just dump the CSV straight into Anki and you're good to go. She dumped all the files into the folder without sorting them. Just dump everything in there and we'll sort it out later.
# ---------------------------------------- # 席並べる / swing by / close call # ----------------------------------------
I'll ask if we can push the seats together. I'll check with them to see if we can arrange the seats next to each other. I'll see if they can squeeze us in together. That was close! We should've asked ahead of time. That was a close call — it would have been better to check in advance. Phew, that was a close one! Should've just asked beforehand. That was a close call — I almost missed the last train.
# ---------------------------------------- # 二郎系ラーメン # ----------------------------------------
At Jiro-style ramen places, right before your bowl is served, the staff asks you "Do you want garlic?" The real answer is something like "extra vegetables, extra garlic!" That's basically the magic spell you have to cast. It's not really a normal conversation — it's more like a ritual unique to the place. Between that and the insane portion sizes, a lot of Japanese people find it kind of intimidating to walk in for the first time. But the food is seriously good, so there are tons of repeat customers. That's Jiro-style ramen for you. At Jiro-style ramen restaurants, just before your ramen is served, a staff member asks, "Would you like garlic?" The expected response is something along the lines of "extra vegetables and extra garlic, please." This has become something of a ritualistic phrase — almost an incantation unique to these establishments. The unconventional ordering system, combined with the overwhelming portion sizes, makes many Japanese people hesitant to visit for the first time. Nevertheless, the flavor is exceptional, which explains the loyal following these places have built up. So at these Jiro-style ramen spots, right before your bowl hits the counter, the guy behind it goes, "You want garlic?" The move is to rattle off something like "extra veg, extra garlic, extra everything!" It's basically a spell you have to learn. The whole thing isn't really a conversation — it's more like a secret code. Even a lot of Japanese people are too intimidated to set foot in one. But man, once you try it, the flavor hits different. These places always have their die-hard regulars. That's the Jiro ramen experience. It depends on the place too — some spots only ask "garlic?" but you're still expected to rattle off a bunch of toppings. It also varies by location — some places only ask about garlic, yet customers are still expected to specify a number of toppings in their response. And it's different at every place too — some just go "garlic?" but you're somehow supposed to know to rattle off like five other things on top of that.
# ---------------------------------------- # 食べすぎ・また来てしまう # ----------------------------------------
Every time I eat too much, I tell myself I'm never coming back — but then I completely forget how stuffed I was and end up coming back anyway. Jiro-style ramen has this weird charm to it. Each time I overindulge, I swear I'll never return — yet somehow I always forget the discomfort and find myself coming back again. There's an inexplicable charm to Jiro-style ramen. Every single time I eat way too much, I'm like "never again" — and then somehow I completely blank on how destroyed I felt and just end up back there. Jiro ramen has this weird pull to it that I can't explain.
# ---------------------------------------- # 二郎・席・スペル教える # ----------------------------------------
Also, unlike regular restaurants, you might not get to sit together at a Jiro-style place — they seat you as spots open up, so we could end up apart. If that happens, don't worry, I'll help you out with the spell! One thing to keep in mind — unlike regular restaurants, Jiro-style places seat customers individually as seats become available, so we may not be able to sit together. Don't worry — I'll make sure to assist you with the ordering ritual. Jiro places don't let you pick your seats, they just fill in as people sit down, so we might end up separated. But don't sweat it, I'll coach you through the spell when the time comes!
# ---------------------------------------- # 若い・期待の星 # ----------------------------------------
Wow, you're so young — you've got a bright future ahead of you! At such a young age, you're certainly someone with a lot of promise. That young? You're totally the one to watch! She's definitely the one to watch in this year's tournament. He's got a bright future ahead of him.
# ---------------------------------------- # 気に入ってもらえてよかった # ----------------------------------------
I'm glad you liked it! Let's go again sometime. I'm really pleased to hear you enjoyed it! We should definitely go again. So glad you're into it! We're totally going back. I'm glad you enjoyed the food — we'll have to go back sometime.
# ---------------------------------------- # 先生に詰められた / ラボ # ----------------------------------------
My professor got on my case about not making enough progress. My supervisor expressed concern about the lack of progress in my work. My professor chewed me out for not getting anywhere with my work. I'll be at the lab working, so come over whenever works for you. I'll be working at the lab, so feel free to come by whenever it's convenient for you. I'll be at the lab, so just swing by whenever you're ready. I just got to the lab. Just got here!
# ---------------------------------------- # chubby / 体型表現 # ----------------------------------------
He was a chubby kid but grew up to be really tall and slim. She was a chubby toddler with the cutest round cheeks. He's always been a bit heavyset but very athletic.
# ---------------------------------------- # はるなを誘う # ----------------------------------------
I don't think she'll come, but should we invite Haruna anyway? She should be upstairs. I have a feeling she might not come, but it might be worth reaching out to Haruna just in case — I believe she's upstairs. She probably won't show, but wanna give Haruna a shout anyway? She should be upstairs. Give me a shout when you're on your way.
# ---------------------------------------- # notice O C / I can feel O doing # ----------------------------------------
I've noticed my English intuition growing at a remarkable pace. I can feel my English brain developing really fast. I can feel my English getting more natural every day. I noticed the gap between thinking and speaking getting smaller. I've started to feel more confident when I speak. She noticed her confidence growing with each conversation.
# ---------------------------------------- # I'm heading to / head as verb # ----------------------------------------
I'm heading to the station — see you there! I'm heading home now. Let's head back before it gets dark. I'm heading out — do you need anything? She's heading for Osaka on Friday. We should head out soon if we want to make it on time.
# ---------------------------------------- # reach out / open up / trust with / that personal # ----------------------------------------
Feel free to reach out to me anytime. I'll reach out to her tomorrow and see what she thinks. She finally opened up about her struggles. He opened up to me for the first time. Thank you for trusting me with something so personal. She trusted him with her biggest secret. I really appreciate you sharing something that personal with me. I didn't know it was that serious.
# ---------------------------------------- # prior engagement / other commitments # ----------------------------------------
I'm sorry, I have a prior engagement that evening. Sorry, I can't make it — I have other commitments. I already have plans that night. I'd love to come, but I have a prior engagement.
# ---------------------------------------- # 複合フレーズ・口語まとめ # ----------------------------------------
Don't sweat it — I'll take care of everything. She coached me through the whole process step by step. I'll coach you through it — just follow my lead. Swing by whenever you're free — I'll be here all afternoon. We ended up staying until midnight. It turned out to be one of the best nights we've had. That place has a weird pull to it — I keep going back even though I can't explain why. I completely blanked on her name when I introduced her. Ramen always hits different when you're really hungry. The restaurant has its die-hard regulars who come every single week.

item.title: 英語学習 listening_20260626
item.description:
# glossary / grocery の発音と使い方
# glossary（用語集）
There's a glossary at the back of the book if you don't know what a word means. Check the glossary if you come across any unfamiliar terms. The textbook has a detailed glossary at the end of each chapter.
# grocery（食料雑貨・食料品店）
I need to stop by the grocery store on my way home. She picked up a few things at the grocery store. We're running low on groceries — I'll go shopping this afternoon.
# 発音の対比
ここがポイントです。 glossary は /ɡlɒs.ər.i/、grocery は /ɡroʊs.ər.i/ で、語頭の子音連結と強勢母音がまったく異なります。
glossary grocery
# 例文での対比
I found the word in the glossary. I found some fresh vegetables at the grocery store.

item.title: 英語学習 listening_20260723
item.description:
# ======================================== # Listening Material — 2026/07/23 # ========================================
# 昨夜寝るのが遅くなって、今朝二度寝しそうになったけど起きられた
I ended up staying up late last night, and this morning I almost fell back asleep — but I managed to get up. I'm actually proud of myself.
I stayed up quite late last night and nearly hit the snooze button this morning, but I managed to get myself up. I can feel myself growing.
I was up way too late last night, and this morning I was so close to going back to sleep — but nope, I dragged myself up. Growth, baby.
# 帰り際の一言：研究室の友人への声かけ
Alright, I'm heading home! I'm going to head home now. Okay, I'm out! See ya!
# ここがポイントです。 # "I'm going to home" は誤り。home は副詞なので to は不要です。 # "go home" / "head home" がセットで正しい形です。
I'm heading home. I'm going to head home now.
# 関連表現まとめ
I'm heading home. I'm out! Alright, I'm out! See ya! I'll be heading out for the day. Okay, I'm out! See ya!

item.title: 英語学習 listening_20260723_2
item.description:
# 相：進行相と完了相の違い（受動態）
He grumbled to his daughter about how he was being treated. The workers contended that they were being exploited by the company. He was being treated poorly by the staff. He had been treated poorly before things changed.
ここがポイントです。 前者は継続している仕打ちのプロセス、後者は区切りのついた過去の経験を表しています。
He was being treated poorly by the staff.
# 受動態の作り方
They treated him badly. He was treated badly. He was being treated badly. He had been treated badly.
ここがポイントです。 受動態は能動態の時制・相をそのままbe動詞に引き継ぎます。単純過去はwas treated、進行相はwas being treated、完了相はhad been treatedになります。
He was being treated badly.
# sinceと基準点の作り方
He entered the nursing home several months ago. Since then, he had been grumbling to his daughter about how he was being treated. He grumbled to his daughter about how they had been treating him poorly since he entered the nursing home.
ここがポイントです。 sinceの後ろに来る出来事はそれ自体が基準点になるため、単純過去を使います。もう一つ前の基準点を作る必要はありません。
He grumbled to his daughter about how they had been treating him poorly since he entered the nursing home.
# 相12マス基本形
I cook. I am cooking. I have cooked. I have been cooking for three hours. I cooked. I was cooking. I had cooked. I had been cooking. I will cook. I will be cooking. I will have cooked. I will have been cooking.
# 時の副詞節の中は現在形
I had already cooked dinner before he arrived. I'll have cooked by the time he comes at 7 PM. I'll have been cooking for hours when he comes. We will have already left by the time she arrives at the airport.
ここがポイントです。 when, before, by the timeなどの時の副詞節は、未来のことでも現在形を使います。willを重ねないのがルールです。
I'll have cooked by the time he comes at 7 PM.
# 実践例文：料理
I cook breakfast every morning at 6 AM. I'm cooking in the kitchen right now. I've just cooked dinner, and it's on the table now. I've been cooking for three hours. I cooked pasta last night. I was cooking when the phone rang at 8 PM yesterday. I'd cooked before he came. I'd been cooking for hours when he arrived. I will cook dinner tomorrow. I'll be cooking at 7 PM tomorrow.
# 実践例文：勉強・待つ・到着
She studies for three hours every night. I'm studying in the library. I've finished studying for the exam. He had been studying for hours. She has been waiting for an hour. I had been waiting for an hour when he came. The train will arrive at 3 PM tomorrow.
# 確信度の助動詞（Modality）
I'll be studying at this time tomorrow. I should be studying at this time tomorrow. I might be studying at this time tomorrow. I'll probably be studying at this time tomorrow.
ここがポイントです。 willは確信度の高い予測、shouldはやや弱い確信、might/couldは五分五分の推測を表します。willをそのまま使いながらprobablyを足すのも有効です。
I'll probably be studying at this time tomorrow.
# 期間・基準点表現
I've been waiting for two hours. I've been waiting since 2 PM. I fell asleep during the meeting. I was in the meeting for two hours. I had finished cooking before he arrived. After he arrived, I served dinner. I will have finished cooking by the time he arrives. I will have finished cooking by 7 PM. I had already left when he called. I hadn't finished the report until midnight. I've lived here since 2020.
# 語彙：rehashing
We kept rehashing the same argument about who forgot to lock the door, and it went nowhere.
# SPEAK MODE添削
Thank you for planning out how to study English for me. Thanks so much for helping me put together a study plan.
